version_relationship.relationship: The objective, feasibility characterization, and counting weights are identical. G1 has n <= 3000 and sum(n) <= 10000, so it may inspect every predecessor pair and their O(log n)-length binary-search paths. G2 raises n to 300000 and sum(n) to 1000000, so this quadratic predecessor DP cannot be reused and must be replaced by an increasing-k sweep with LCA-batched transitions.

sources.notes: Read the complete statement, binary-search pseudocode, correctness definition, both samples, and the easy constraints n <= 3000 and sum(n) <= 10000.
Read the rendered full G1 tutorial. It proves the increasing-test characterization, unique optimal kept set per counted array, four-way path-overlap weight, endpoint DP, and O(m^2 log n) approach.
Inspected Hori's Accepted C++20 G1 submission. It explicitly builds each touch path, applies the four transition factors, combines maximum length with tie counts, and completes the suffix count.

Read the full cross-version article filed under G2. It independently models the search as a segment-tree path, reduces compatibility to LIS after deleting the x > 1, k = 1 case, and derives the Easy counting DP and O(n^2 log n)-type bound.

Independently derived the path inequalities, sufficiency construction, transition product, and no-double-count argument. An in-memory check on 1200 random distinct-(x,k) instances with n <= 5 matched brute force, the G1 DP, and the G2 batched recurrence; no files or database rows were written.

annotation.summary: Choose a largest subset of tests (x_i,k_i) that one array over [1,n] can satisfy under the given lower-bound-style binary search, then count all arrays satisfying an optimal-size subset.
annotation.constraints: 1 <= t <= 10000; 1 <= m <= n <= 3000; sum(n) <= 10000; all x_i are distinct and all k_i are distinct; array values lie in [1,n]; answers are modulo 998244353. Time limit 5 seconds, memory limit 256 MB.
annotation.core_idea: Represent the fixed binary search on positions by the balanced interval decision tree: an interval [l,r] stores mid=floor((l+r)/2), with children [l,mid] and [mid+1,r]. The inspected indices for a return position x form its root-to-leaf path P(x). A test (x,k) fixes a_x=k, requires a_p<k for p in P(x) with p<x, and requires a_p>=k for p in P(x) with p>x. A set of tests is feasible exactly when, after sorting by x, its k values are strictly increasing and it excludes (x>1,k=1). Necessity follows at the separating midpoint of any inversion; sufficiency follows by constructing a nondecreasing step array whose first occurrence of each kept k is its x. Hence the maximum kept count is an LIS. For counting, let dp_i=(best length, number of assignments to a_1..a_i) for optimal kept chains ending at test i. For adjacent kept tests j<i with k_j<k_i, constraints from older tests on the gap are weaker than the bound from j, so each position j<p<i is classified using only P(j) and P(i): neither path, only P(j), only P(i), or both, giving respectively n, n-k_j+1, k_i-1, or k_i-k_j choices. Multiply these factors by dp_j, maximize the chain length, and sum counts on ties. Base states count the prefix constraints of one test, and a final analogous factor completes the suffix. Counts of different optimal chains are disjoint: an array satisfying two distinct maximum-size chains would satisfy their union, contradicting maximality.
annotation.complexity: With tests stored compactly by position, path generation and each predecessor transition take O(log n), for O(n + m^2 log n) time and O(n + m log n) memory. The inspected official implementation scans all positions j for each tested endpoint, so its literal bound is O(nm + m^2 log n), still O(n^2 log n) under m <= n. These are conservative bounds including modular exponentiation.
tricks: Turn the binary-search trace into a root-to-leaf path in a fixed balanced interval tree.
Prove compatible tests are exactly an increasing subsequence in k after sorting by x, apart from the impossible x > 1, k = 1 test.
Partition new positions by membership in the two touch paths and multiply the four exact value-range sizes.
Store DP values in a max-length plus sum-on-ties pair.
Use uniqueness of the optimal satisfied-test set for each counted array to avoid inclusion-exclusion.
uncertainty_points: No unresolved correctness issue remains for the primary G1 recurrence after the official tutorial and Accepted code, the independent cross-version Luogu derivation, and the in-memory brute-force comparison.

The dedicated Luogu G1 solution index was consulted but currently lists zero G1-specific articles; the Luogu cross-check is therefore the article filed under G2 that explicitly analyzes both Easy and Hard.

The official tutorial states O(m^2 log n), while the literal Accepted code scans n possible positions for every tested endpoint. The payload records both the compact implementation bound and the conservative source-code bound.
No new taxonomy tag is proposed; dp/lis-dp, dp/counting-dp, graph/tree, and math/combinatorics already cover the reusable content.
proof_risks: Increasing k is not merely necessary. Sufficiency must be proved, for example by a nondecreasing step-array construction with first occurrences at the selected x positions.
The test (x>1,k=1) is impossible because every array value is at least 1, so the binary search for 1 can never take a right branch and always returns position 1.
Counting must constrain only inspected path positions, not force the whole array to be sorted. The sorted step array is an existence proof, not the family being counted.
The reduction to the two adjacent kept tests on a gap needs the path-laminarity argument; otherwise constraints from an older test may appear to have been omitted.
For j<i, the exact factors are n-k_j+1 on P(j) only, k_i-1 on P(i) only, and k_i-k_j on both. Swapping a strict and non-strict inequality changes the answer.
The fixed entries a_j=k_j and a_i=k_i are excluded from the free-position exponents; midpoint endpoint cases must not count them again.
Summing counts over optimal chains is valid only after proving that one array cannot satisfy two distinct maximum-size chains.

solution_variants.name: quadratic-touch-path-counting-lis-dp
solution_variants.summary: Sort tests by position. For every endpoint i, combine all earlier j with k_j<k_i. Scan the O(log n)-length touch paths of j and i to count positions in the four overlap classes, multiply their exact choice counts into dp_j, and retain the maximum chain length with summed tie counts. Add single-test bases and suffix-completion factors.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n + m^2 log n) time with compact test iteration and O(n + m log n) memory; the official Accepted implementation's position scan gives O(nm + m^2 log n), bounded by O(n^2 log n).

tags.evidence: After sorting tests by x, feasibility is exactly strict increase in k, and dp_i maximizes the length of a compatible subsequence ending at i.
Each state stores both the best compatible-chain length and the number of prefix arrays attaining it; transitions multiply assignment counts and sum them on equal-length ties.
Every test is a root-to-leaf path in the implicit balanced binary-search decision tree, and transition weights are determined by intersections of two such paths.
The array count is a product of independent value-range sizes for free and path-constrained positions, followed by a disjoint sum over optimal chains.
